humanities_role_explanation: The paper uses 19th-century historical newspaper texts containing domain-specific linguistic features (e.g., historical vocabulary, archaic currency formats, old spelling conventions) to evaluate how generative LLMs can improve document accessibility for digital humanities scholars.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluates models solely on text sequences (mapping corrupted OCR output to clean, human-transcribed ground truth text).
analytical_tasks_explanation: The tasks focus on sequence-to-sequence correction of OCR-induced character errors (substitutions, insertions, deletions) within a historical context, requiring the preservation of archaic lexical styles and restoration of corrupted named entities (e.g., people, organizations).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: The evaluation is restricted to the specific task of post-OCR correction on historical newspaper texts (BLN600).

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates Llama 2 (7B and 13B models) and compares them with BART.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Llama 2 is the primary generative LLM being instruction-tuned and evaluated.

arts_humanities_data.note: Uses BLN600, containing 19th-century British newspaper articles (History, Language and Linguistics).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates using Character Error Rate (CER) reduction percentage based on Levenshtein distance.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the BLN600 dataset consists of 19th-century English-language newspapers.

summary: The paper explores the application of generative LLMs for post-OCR correction in historical digital archives, using the BLN600 dataset of 19th-century British newspapers. It compares instruction-tuned Llama 2 models against a traditionally fine-tuned BART model, highlighting the superior performance of LLMs in low-data scenarios.
key_findings: Instruction-tuned Llama 2 13B achieved a 54.51% reduction in Character Error Rate (CER) on the test set, substantially outperforming the fine-tuned BART Large model (23.30% reduction). Llama 2 also demonstrated much stronger performance when training data was severely limited.
